channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: The Maine Town That Actually Wants a Data Center
item.description: This year, Maine nearly became the first state to pass a statewide moratorium on new data centers. But before the law could take effect, supporters of an A.I. data center project in the small town of Jay rallied to fight the ban — and won. So why do residents there want one? We traveled to Jay to find out.
item.dc:creator: Jeremy Raff, Zach Caldwell and Ben Laffin

item.title: California Will Vote on a Billionaire Tax. Billionaires Aren’t Happy.
item.description: The wealthy are fighting back with two ballot issues that could nullify the measure in November. The battle reflects a broader tension as American politics contends with deep inequality.
item.dc:creator: Laurel Rosenhall
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: California voters will confront ballot issues designed to cancel one another.

item.title: Trump Issues Order to Reduce Pesticides in Food as Kennedy Allies Fume
item.description: The order, which calls for studying the health risks of pesticides in the food supply, does not involve new federal funding, and does not call for regulations or legislation.
item.dc:creator: Sheryl Gay Stolberg
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump held a Rose Garden dinner with American farmers on Thursday, the same day he issued an executive order aimed at reducing pesticides in the food supply.

item.title: Johnson Says He Will Send Housing Bill to Trump
item.description: After meeting with the president, the speaker said he would send him a housing bill that Mr. Trump declined to sign this week. There was no word on whether he would sign it.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Speaker Mike Johnson did not say on Thursday whether President Trump had promised to sign the housing bill or whether the two had even discussed it.

item.title: How Trump Wants to Upend Voting in America
item.description: President Trump is demanding that Congress pass the SAVE America Act to change American elections. Our national politics reporter Nick Corasaniti looks at what’s in it.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti, Laura Bult, Jon Miller, Nikolay Nikolov, Stephanie Swart, Rafaela Balster and Gilad Thaler

item.title: Obama Says He Occupies a ‘Suite’ in Trump’s Head
item.description: Former President Barack Obama says President Trump has an obsession with him and that Mr. Trump “knows better” than to say “crazy stuff” to his face.
item.dc:creator: Erica L. Green
item.category: Obama, Barack H, Presidential Library and Museum
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Former President Barack Obama at the opening of the Obama Presidential Center in Chicago last week.

item.title: He Was Deported Back to Venezuela and Started Anew. Then the Quakes Hit.
item.description: Venezuelans in South Florida were frantically trying to track down friends and relatives after two earthquakes in Venezuela. Jorge Erazo was among the missing.
item.dc:creator: David Ovalle
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Civilians and emergency medical workers looking for survivors in the rubble of a building that collapsed during an earthquake in Catia La Mar, Venezuela.

item.title: Military Leaders Sought Flu Vaccination Program Weeks Before Outbreak Hit Base
item.description: Officials anticipated a problem soon after Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth lifted the vaccine requirement in April.
item.dc:creator: Greg Jaffe
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
Lackland Air Force Base (San Antonio, Tex)
item.media:credit: Lynsey Addario for The New York Times
item.media:description: Marine recruits during basic training at Parris Island in South Carolina in 2019.

item.title: Colorado Brothers Hid Decaying Bodies in Their Funeral Home, Authorities Say
item.description: The owners of Davis Mortuary in Pueblo, Colo., face multiple charges after state inspectors said they found two dozen decomposing bodies behind a concealed door. One of the owners is a former coroner.
item.dc:creator: Mark Walker
item.media:credit: Jerilee Bennett/The Gazette, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Hazmat crew members from the Colorado State Highway Patrol preparing to enter the mortuary in Pueblo, Colo., in August 2025.

item.title: John Stockwell, Who Wrote a Tell-All Book About the C.I.A., Dies at 88
item.description: A former covert operative, he published his resignation letter in The Washington Post and went on to write “In Search of Enemies,” a book the agency sought to suppress.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel
item.category: In Search of Enemies: A CIA Story (Book)
item.media:credit: Jack Manning/The New York Times
item.media:description: John Stockwell in 1978, the year he published “In Search of Enemies,” his C.I.A. exposé. The New York Times Book Review described it as “an extremely useful account” of the agency’s “political and military failure.”

item.title: Justices Clash on Whether Race Played a Role in Trump’s Bid to Deport Haitians
item.description: The split mirrored one that has long divided Americans: how seriously to take the president’s loose, provocative and sometimes ugly remarks.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: Justice Samuel A. Alito Jr.’s majority opinion did not include any examples of President Trump’s statements about Haitians.

item.title: Complaint Seeks Disclosure of Pentagon’s Agreement With Scouting America
item.description: The Pentagon and the organization have given contradictory accounts of an agreement reached in February.
item.dc:creator: Helene Cooper
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said that Scouting America was banning transgender people from openly participating in the group. The group said it had not changed its policies.

item.title: Here’s What the Supreme Court’s Decision Means for TPS Holders
item.description: The ruling on Temporary Protection Status will have swift repercussions for migrants and employers alike.
item.dc:creator: Jazmine Ulloa, Lydia DePillis and Miriam Jordan
item.media:credit: Maddie McGarvey for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Haitian family receives help obtaining passports and documents at the St. Vincent de Paul center in Springfield, Ohio, this year.

item.title: U.S. Military’s Weapons Shortage Shows Few Signs of Easing Soon
item.description: President Trump and Pentagon officials tried to reassure manufacturers as they sought additional funding from Congress.
item.dc:creator: Helene Cooper and Farah Stockman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: T.J. Kirkpatrick for The New York Times
item.media:description: Raytheon missiles at a trade show in Virginia in 2023. The company said in February it would increase production of Tomahawk missiles but needed to find additional suppliers.

item.title: Inside the C.D.C.’s Mad Scramble to Meet Kennedy’s Demands
item.description: A cache of internal emails offers a look at the pressure the nation’s public health officials faced from the new health secretary in the early months of the Trump administration.
item.dc:creator: Sheryl Gay Stolberg
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: Robert F. Kennedy Jr., seated, at a Senate confirmation hearing last year.

item.title: Trump Keeps Undercutting Republicans’ Message, Squandering His Own Trifecta
item.description: Divisions between the president and his party on Capitol Hill have muddled Republicans’ midterm pitch to voters, and have crippled the G.O.P. at what should be the peak of its power.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump with Senate Republicans at the Capitol on Wednesday.

item.title: What Are the Paths Forward for the Housing Bill After Trump Refused to Sign It?
item.description: President Trump abruptly scrapped plans to sign a major housing bill. It could still become law, with or without a presidential signature. But Mr. Trump could also try to kill it.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Wu and Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Ashley Wu/The New York Times

item.title: ‘Nowhere to Go’: T.P.S. Ruling Plunges Many Migrants Into Limbo
item.description: The decision renders 1.3 million people from more than a dozen countries, many who have lived in the United States for decades, vulnerable to deportation.
item.dc:creator: Miriam Jordan, Andrea Zarate and Jazmine Ulloa
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Members of the National T.P.S. Alliance held a rally in Pasadena, Calif., on Wednesday ahead of the Supreme Court ruling on legal protections under Temporary Protected Status.

item.title: Federal Judge Strikes Key Parts of Trump Order Restricting Mail Voting
item.description: The ruling rejected the Trump administration’s attempt to change federal election procedures through an executive order.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti and Adam Sella
item.media:credit: Justin Sullivan/Getty Images
item.media:description: “The Constitution does not grant the President any specific powers over elections,” a federal judge ruled on Thursday, tossing crucial parts of President Trump’s executive order that sought to restrict mail voting.

item.title: Frank Guarini, Seven-Term New Jersey Representative, Dies at 101
item.description: A reform-minded Democrat, he served on the tax-writing House Ways and Means Committee and offered a pragmatic mix of progressive legislation and conservative fiscal philosophy.
item.dc:creator: Dennis Hevesi
item.category: House Committee on Ways and Means
item.media:credit: Laura Patterson/CQ Roll Call, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Frank J. Guarini in 1992. Though he was known in Congress for his reserve and willingness to negotiate, he relished a bit of contention with New York politicians.

item.title: Supreme Court Rejects Lawsuit Against Bayer Alleging Roundup Weedkiller Caused Cancer
item.description: The court’s decision is likely to determine the future of thousands of lawsuits against Bayer, which manufactures the weedkiller, over similar claims.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.media:credit: Josh Edelson/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Roundup, which was created in the 1970s, is one of the most popular weedkillers in the world.

item.title: Supreme Court Overturns Hawaii Gun Law
item.description: The case involved a Second Amendment challenge to a Hawaii law that barred carrying concealed weapons without permission onto private property open to the public.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.category: New York State Rifle & Pistol Association v Bruen (Supreme Court Decision)
New York State Rifle and Pistol Assn
item.media:credit: Alyssa Schukar for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jason Wolford and Atom Kasprzycki were among the plaintiffs who sued to overturn the Hawaii gun law.

item.title: Supreme Court Expands Trump’s Power Over Immigration With TPS and Southern Border Decisions
item.description: The court handed President Trump victories in his push to rescind deportation protections for hundreds of thousands of people and turn away migrants at the southern border.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: The Supreme Court’s 6-to-3 decision in two cases with broad implications divided along ideological lines, with the liberal justices dissenting.

item.title: Supreme Court Allows Trump to Block Asylum Seekers at Border
item.description: A policy of turning back asylum seekers at the border was rescinded in 2021, but the Trump administration wants the flexibility to reinstate it as a tool for border control.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: Paul Ratje for The New York Times
item.media:description: Migrants approaching the U.S.-Mexico border in Ciudad Juárez, Mexico, last year. The case before the Supreme Court revolved around what it means to “arrive” in the United States.

item.title: Here’s What It Means to Be a Democratic Socialist
item.description: Universal health care, taxing the wealthy and opposition to military aid to Israel are among the movement’s key tenets.
item.dc:creator: Emily Davies
item.media:credit: Graham Dickie for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Democratic Socialists of America helped organize a “Tax the Rich” rally in February in Albany, N.Y.

item.title: Senate Democrats Will Train Staff as Election Observers
item.description: Senator Chuck Schumer, the minority leader, said the program was aimed at counteracting potential efforts by the Trump administration to manipulate elections.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold

item.title: Bible Passages May Soon Be Required Reading in Texas Public Schools
item.description: Texas is set to pass what may be the first state-mandated book list for public school students. It focuses on classic literature and includes Bible excerpts.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Mervosh
item.media:credit: Sandy Huffaker for The New York Times
item.media:description: Most book lists are set by individual teachers or schools.

item.title: M.I.T. Didn’t Publish an Antisemitism Report. A Professor Wrote His Own.
item.description: Now, the professor is in a fight with the university over whether he can use M.I.T. money to promote a book critical of the school.
item.dc:creator: Mark Arsenault
item.media:credit: Erica Denhoff/Icon Sportswire, via Associated Press Images
item.media:description: The Trump administration has seized on reports of campus antisemitism to pressure colleges.
